1.synthesis:
Moving outward from the most holy place, the divine instructions now address the courtyard's central piece: the great altar of burnt offering. Made of acacia wood overlaid with bronze, it measured five cubits square and three cubits high (Exod 27:1). At each of its four corners rose a horn — projections of the same structure — and all its utensils (pots, shovels, basins, forks, firepans) were likewise of bronze (Exod 27:3). A bronze grating ran halfway down the interior, with a bronze ring at each corner for the carrying-poles (Exod 27:4-5). The altar was hollow — framed boards — so that fire could burn from within (Exod 27:8).

This altar was the first object encountered in approaching the tabernacle from the east. Before the worshipper could proceed to the holy place, he passed by the great altar where the sacrifices were burned. Its position was not incidental: it declared that access to God required sacrifice. No one moved deeper into the sanctuary without first acknowledging the reality of sin and the necessity of substitutionary death.

Gill observes that the altar was a type of the cross of Christ: "It is the altar on which Christ offered himself as a burnt offering without spot to God." The four horns (projections at the corners) to which animals were bound for sacrifice (Ps 118:27) were also the points touched with blood in atonement (Exod 29:12). Matthew Henry notes: "The altar was to be hollow, that it might be light for carriage; this is applicable to the cross of Christ, which was not a burdensome thing but that by which the believer is crucified to the world."

The bronze of the altar — in contrast to the gold of the inner sanctuary — signals the altar's place in the holiness gradient. Bronze withstands fire; it is the appropriate material for the place where judgment is executed. All Israel's offerings — burnt, peace, sin, and grain — were consumed on this altar throughout the wilderness years and, later, in Solomon's temple.
1.historical_context:
The altar's dimensions (five cubits square, three cubits high — roughly 7.5 feet square, 4.5 feet tall) placed it as the largest single piece of furniture in the entire tabernacle complex. Its hollow construction of boards overlaid with bronze was practical for portability; a solid bronze altar would have been impossibly heavy. The grating halfway down may have allowed ash to fall while supporting burning wood above. The four horns were so characteristic of Israelite altars that Amos uses "horns of the altar" as a metonym for the altar itself (Amos 3:14).

Archaeologically, horned altars have been found at Beersheba, Megiddo, and other Israelite sites — typically much smaller, for incense or grain offerings. The great burnt-offering altar in the tabernacle and temple was of a scale unlike the household or private altars recovered by archaeology, befitting its public, national function.
1.literary_structure: The altar specification (vv 1-8) forms a self-contained unit with the pattern of other furnishing passages: materials, dimensions, accessories, carrying provisions, and concluding authority ("as it has been shown you on the mountain," v 8). It is notably brief compared to the ark or lampstand, perhaps because the altar's significance lay not in its complexity but in its function — the place of fire, blood, and substitutionary death.
1.theological_application:
That the altar stands at the entrance to the sanctuary — the first object encountered, impossible to bypass — establishes the irreversible sequence of biblical soteriology: atonement precedes access. No priest entered the holy place without passing the smoking altar; no worshipper brought his gift without watching it consumed there. Matthew Henry reflects: "We cannot come to God without an altar, a sacrifice." The New Testament reaffirms this: "without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness of sins" (Heb 9:22). The altar's centrality to the courtyard is the architectural expression of that truth.

The hollow construction speaks to how the gospel works: the cross is not a burden but the very thing by which "the world has been crucified to me, and I to the world" (Gal 6:14). The believer who takes up the cross is not weighed down but liberated.
1.christology: Gill's identification of the altar with the cross is the church's deepest reading of this passage. The burnt offering — wholly consumed, ascending as a rêaḥ nîḥōaḥ, a pleasing aroma to God (Lev 1:9) — finds its ultimate referent in Christ, "who loved us and gave himself up for us, a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God" (Eph 5:2). The bronze — which withstands fire — points to the one who bore the full fire of divine judgment and was not consumed by it but passed through death into resurrection life. The four horns at the altar's corners, touching all points of the compass, declare that the atoning work of the cross is universal in scope: "the propitiation for our sins, and not for ours only but also for the sins of the whole world" (1 John 2:2).

9.synthesis:
The courtyard formed the public perimeter of Israel's sanctuary. One hundred cubits long (east-west) and fifty cubits wide (north-south), it was enclosed by linen curtains five cubits high hung on bronze-socketed posts with silver hooks and rods (Exod 27:9-15). The eastern end, where worshippers entered, had a gate-screen twenty cubits wide of richly colored embroidered linen (Exod 27:16). All the courtyard's hardware — pegs, posts, bases — was bronze, distinguishing it from the silver and gold of the interior. The structure was entirely open to the sky.

The courtyard was where the congregation of Israel could come. They could not enter the holy place — that was for priests only — but they could enter the court, present their offerings at the gate, and witness the priests' work at the altar. It was the people's space within the sacred precincts, the outermost ring of God's dwelling. Gill describes it as "typical of the visible church of God on earth, which, though an enclosure, and is separated from the world, yet consists of professors, good and bad, of real saints and hypocrites."

The height of the linen curtains (five cubits) is significant: tall enough that those outside could not see over them into the court, but the worshippers within could see the blue sky above. The enclosure created a world-within-a-world — separated from the camp, oriented toward the tabernacle at its western end, dominated by the altar at its center.

The eastern gate — one quarter of the east wall's space — was wide enough to permit easy access while still marking clear distinction between inside and outside. The gate's ornate screen, woven in the same colors as the tabernacle's entrance curtain, gave those approaching a visual foretaste of the sanctuary's beauty before they crossed the threshold.
9.historical_context:
The courtyard of 100 × 50 cubits was a substantial public space — roughly 150 by 75 feet — containing the altar and laver (described later in ch 30). The standard military camp of Israel was organized concentrically around the tabernacle, with the three Levitical clans camping immediately adjacent and the twelve tribes arranged by banner at greater distance (Num 2). The courtyard was therefore the innermost sacred zone accessible to non-priests. Its bronze hardware, compared to gold and silver within, reflects not merely functional durability but symbolic gradation: those who approach only the courtyard have not yet passed through to the gold of priestly service.

The gate's twenty-cubit width on a fifty-cubit east wall provided a generous entrance — 40% of the wall — suggesting that God's intention was accessibility for the entire congregation, not restriction. The colored screen at the gate mirrored the tabernacle entrance screen, creating visual continuity from the outermost approach to the inner sanctuary.
9.literary_structure: The courtyard specification (vv 9-19) completes the outward movement of chs 25-27: ark → furniture → curtains → frames → veil → altar → courtyard. Each successive passage moves one step further from the most holy center. The courtyard is the final element, forming the outer boundary of sacred space. The passage ends at v 19 with a summary of all the tabernacle's bronze hardware, and then v 20 introduces the new section on the oil for the lamps — a transition from structure to service.
9.theological_application:
Gill's identification of the courtyard with the visible church is apt: a space that is separated from the world yet contains both genuine worshippers and those who only appear to belong. The parable of the wheat and tares (Matt 13:24-30) describes precisely the visible church's mixed reality. The courtyard was not the most holy place, but it was genuinely holy — within the sacred perimeter, under the shadow of the tabernacle, within earshot of the priests' service. Matthew Henry applies this to the gathered congregation: "The court was open above; the worshippers in it could see the heavens, for thither their worship was directed."

The linen curtains — the same material as the inner tabernacle covering — gave the courtyard a quality of purity that demarcated it sharply from the surrounding camp. Even the outer boundary of the church is to be characterized by holiness, not merely by organizational form.
9.christology:
The courtyard's eastern gate — the single point of entry — anticipates Christ's declaration: "I am the door; if anyone enters by me, he will be saved and will go in and out and find pasture" (John 10:9). There was one gate in the eastern wall; there is one mediator between God and humanity (1 Tim 2:5). The gate's ornate beauty — embroidered in the same blue, purple, crimson, and white as the inner sanctuary — spoke to those approaching: the way in is beautiful, not grim; the entrance is through grace, not merit.

The courtyard's open sky above may speak to the resurrection: access to the living God is not sealed in a closed chamber but opens upward, toward the heavens where Christ has ascended and from which he will return (Acts 1:11). The worshipper in the courtyard looks both inward (toward the tabernacle, where God dwells) and upward (to heaven, where God reigns) — the double orientation of the Christian life.

20.synthesis:
Two verses close the chapter, yet their brevity belies their weight. The congregation of Israel is commanded to bring pure beaten olive oil for the lampstand, so that its lamps might burn continually (Exod 27:20). Aaron and his sons are to tend this flame in the tent of meeting, before the veil that guards the ark — "a statute forever to be observed throughout their generations by the people of Israel" (Exod 27:21).

The olive oil was to be the finest quality: zak kātît, pure and beaten — oil pressed from olives crushed in a mortar rather than extracted in a press, yielding cleaner, clearer oil that burned without smoke or residue. Nothing inferior was acceptable for the light before the Lord. Keil and Delitzsch note that the lampstand was never to be extinguished: "to cause the lamp to burn always." In the inner sanctuary, where no natural light penetrated, the lampstand's perpetual flame was the only light.

That the whole congregation provided the oil while only the priests tended the lamps divides corporate provision from priestly ministry. Israel as a people sustained the light; the Aaronic sons set and trimmed it. Both roles were necessary; neither was sufficient alone. Gill observes that this passage "begins a new section of the law," transitioning from the structure of the sanctuary to its regular maintenance — the ongoing service that kept the dwelling of God functioning.

Matthew Henry notes that the oil for the lamp was a "statute forever" — not a one-time provision but a perpetual obligation. The light in the holy place was not self-sustaining; it required constant human contribution of the best the land could produce. The church's light is likewise not self-generating: it requires the continual offering of the finest of what believers possess — prayer, Scripture, worship — to keep the flame alive.
20.historical_context:
Beaten olive oil was the most refined form available in the ancient world, requiring hand-pounding of olives rather than mechanical pressing. The additional pressing stage removed impurities and produced oil with a higher smoke point and cleaner flame. This was also the most labor-intensive and expensive form; the command to use only this quality for the lampstand placed the holy place's illumination in the highest category of Israel's offerings.

The phrase "from evening to morning" (later specified in Lev 24:3) indicated that the lamps burned through the night — the hours of natural darkness. During the day, the closed tabernacle needed no exterior light source; the inner lamps provided the only illumination at all times. Samuel's calling in the night while "the lamp of God had not yet gone out" (1 Sam 3:3) gives a narrative glimpse of this practice.
20.literary_structure: The passage is transitional: it closes the structural section of chs 25-27 with a note on the service those structures required, and it foreshadows the priestly legislation of chs 28-29. The lampstand was specified in ch 25; only now is its maintenance commanded. Structure and service are always paired in the tabernacle instructions: the object is useless without the ongoing act of consecration that keeps it burning. This two-verse unit anticipates the elaborate daily routines of the priestly calendar to come.
20.theological_application:
Gill and Matthew Henry both note that the lampstand was typical of the church, and its oil typical of the Holy Spirit. The congregation's responsibility to supply the oil corresponds to the church's call to prayer — specifically, to petition continually for the Spirit's presence and power. The parable of the ten virgins (Matt 25:1-13) draws on exactly this imagery: the wise have oil for the long night, the foolish do not. Readiness for Christ's return is measured by whether one has maintained an ongoing supply of the Spirit's grace, not whether one had it once.

The "statute forever" language marks this as a perpetual obligation rather than a seasonal or occasional one. The light of God's presence among his people is to be continuously tended, never allowed to go out through neglect. Churches that cease to pray, to open Scripture, to gather for worship — they are churches that have run out of oil.
20.christology:
The oil for the lampstand connects directly to the Spirit who rests upon Christ without measure (John 3:34). Isaiah's portrait of the Servant — "The Spirit of the Lord God is upon me, because the Lord has anointed me" (Isa 61:1) — identifies Jesus as the true anointed one, whose Spirit-anointing was declared at his baptism (Matt 3:16). The perpetual flame of the lampstand points to the risen Christ in whom the fire of God never goes out, who sends the Spirit without end (John 15:26).

That the lamp burned "before the Lord" from evening to morning speaks to Christ's own unceasing priestly work: "he always lives to make intercession" (Heb 7:25). Through every dark night of the soul — every season when the presence of God seems distant — the lamp continues to burn before the veil by the Spirit's sustaining grace. The believer rests not on the strength of their own oil supply but on the One who is himself "the true light, which gives light to everyone" (John 1:9).
